utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: michelle king , date of birth 01/05/1986 , mrnq27631 . new patient visit for acid reflux . past medical history of anxiety and seasonal allergies .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hello , are you mrs. king ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i am .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i'm dr. phillips . very nice to meet you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you as well .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how can i help you today ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , my primary doctor , dr. scott , he referred me here .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i've just got really bad acid reflux .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . can you tell me more about it ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah . though i mean i ... right now , i take 40 milligrams of omeprazole twice a day . some days , it is fine , but others are just really bad .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: what do you feel ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it feels like burning .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: in your chest ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , in my chest and also in my throat .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: in your throat and chest . all right . any difficulty with swallowing ? feels like food or liquids kinda get stuck ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . that's good . any abdominal pain ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , not really .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: nausea or vomiting , changes in bowel movements ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: constipation , diarrhea , rectal bleeding ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nope .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . very good . and how long have you had this problem ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i've had acid reflux since i was around 12 , but it has gotten worse over the past few years .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and how long have you been on the omeprazole ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: on the way , it is now i think about a year .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . do you find that it's done anything for you in the past ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean , i can tell it's helped because some days , i'll be fine , and then some days , i eat something and it feels terrible .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: are there any things that make your symptoms worse that you can pinpoint , like food choices or stress ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sure . probably stress and food choices sometimes .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . what things bother you ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: spicy foods , um , so i try not to eat them too often , but sometimes even drinking a soda can cause it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: we do know that caffeine and caffeinated beverages will do that , even sodas . um , even just the carbonation can cause that . how about tomato-based products or citrus products ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , sometimes , but i still eat them .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . it's reassuring that you are n't having any difficulty swallowing , though . how's your weight been ? pretty stable ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'd say pretty stable , although i have n't had much of an appetite for the past two months . i have been under a lot of work stress .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: what do you do for work ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm a paralegal .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and any family history of gi issues ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , my grandmother had esophageal cancer .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: on your mom or dad's side ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mom's side . and my mom had some issues like acid reflux , and she was taking some medication that coats the lining of your stomach .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . have you ever had an upper endoscopy ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i think maybe when i was younger .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: but not in the last 10 years ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: have you ever tried any medication other than omeprazole ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , at one point i was on omeprazole and ranititine -dine . it did help . but other than that , those are the only medications i have taken .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . have a seat here , and let me listen to your heart and lungs , and then we can talk about a plan for you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so normal physical exam per my new patient template , except for well-healed abdominal surgical scars noted . what's the surgical scar here on your abdomen from ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i always forget about that surgery . i had it when i was a baby . my mom said my intestines were twisted , so i had to have surgery when i was around one month old .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hm , gotcha . okay . so let's talk about what you have going on . from what you've told me , it sounds like you've had a several-year history of gerd , which is the medical term for acid reflux . and recently your symptoms have been poorly controlled with the omeprazole , 40 milligrams twice daily . you've been having heartburn despite taking this medication , correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and you've identified trigger foods like spicy foods , carbonated bev- beverages , citrus , and tomato-based foods , but you have n't cut these foods out of your diet completely yet . so the first thing i'd try is changing your diet because these foods can exacerbate your gerd , even though you're taking the ppi .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , yeah , i could definitely do a better job of avoiding those foods .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: great . so stress is another trigger that we need to address . high stress can cause worsening of your symptoms .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , stress and anxiety are definitely big problems for me .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so working on that may help with your gerd . additionally , we can consider trying a different ppi since the only one you've tried up to this point is omeprazole . if you're okay with this , we can switch your omeprazole to nexium .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that's fine with me . i'm definitely open to trying whatever .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: perfect . i will send a prescription for nexium 40 milligrams twice daily to your pharmacy on file . i'll also recommend that you have a procedure called a egd , or upper endoscopy , to look for potential causes of the gerd . we'd sedate you and then pass a small camera through your mouth , down your throat , through your stomach , and end in the small intestines . we'd look for any ulcers or signs of precancerous or cancerous conditions that we can take biopsies at the same time if we see anything .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay . can i schedule that today ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah . you can get scheduled at checkout desk . i'll have you follow up with me in the office for the egd so we can discuss the results and talk about how you're doing with the dietary , stress , and medication changes . how does all of that sound ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that sounds good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: perfect . i will walk you to the checkout desk . be sure to call my office and let me know if anything changes or if you have problems before your egd and follow-up appointment .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: please insert the procedure paragraph in the plan . i suspect that she likely has gerd with underlying functional etiology as well . reassuringly , she has no unintentional weight loss and denies any dysphagia . if her egd is normal and her symptoms pers- persist despite a trial of nexium , we can consider a trial of protonix and obtain a ph-impedance study . this will allow us to rule out abnormal acid reflux exposure and to decipher between a functional etiology and hypersensitivity .

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 35-year-old female presenting for a new patient evaluation of acid reflux with a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.
Acid reflux
The patient takes omeprazole 40 mg twice daily.
The patient describes her symptoms as a burning sensation.
The doctor asks if the burning sensation is in the chest.
The patient confirms the burning sensation is in her chest and throat.
The doctor asks if the patient has difficulty swallowing.
The patient denies difficulty swallowing.
The doctor asks if the patient has abdominal pain.
The patient denies abdominal pain.
The doctor asks about nausea, vomiting, and changes in bowel movements.
The patient denies nausea, vomiting, and changes in bowel movements.
The doctor asks about constipation, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding.
The patient denies constipation, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding.
The doctor asks about the duration of the symptoms.
The patient has had acid reflux since age 12, with worsening symptoms over the past few years.
The doctor asks about the duration of omeprazole use.
The patient has been taking omeprazole for approximately one year.
The doctor asks about triggers for symptoms.
The patient identifies stress and food choices as triggers.
The patient identifies spicy foods and soda as triggers.
The doctor asks about tomato-based or citrus products as triggers.
The patient confirms tomato and citrus products are triggers but admits to still consuming them.
The doctor asks about weight stability.
The patient reports stable weight but notes a poor appetite for the past two months due to work stress.
The doctor asks about family history of GI issues.
The patient reports a maternal grandmother with esophageal cancer.
The doctor asks if the patient has ever had an upper endoscopy.
The patient believes she had an endoscopy when she was younger.
The doctor asks if the patient has tried any other medications besides omeprazole.
The patient reports previously taking omeprazole and ranitidine concurrently.
The doctor notes a well-healed abdominal surgical scar during the physical exam.
The doctor asks about the origin of the abdominal surgical scar.
The patient explains the scar is from surgery for twisted intestines when she was one month old.
The doctor recommends dietary modifications to avoid trigger foods.
The doctor recommends stress reduction to help with GERD symptoms.
The doctor proposes switching from omeprazole to Nexium.
The doctor prescribes Nexium 40 mg twice daily.
The doctor recommends an EGD to investigate GERD causes.
The doctor instructs the patient to schedule the EGD and follow up in the office to discuss results.
The doctor instructs the patient to call the office if any changes or problems occur before the procedure.
